_editing_guide.purpose: Structured signals for the tourism vertical. Used by Claude in Stage 1 and the memory system to interpret lead data correctly.

how_to_expand.pain_points: Add any pain point a prospect has mentioned or that comes up repeatedly in calls. Be specific — 'high OTA commission' is better than 'cost issues'.
how_to_expand.positive_signals: Things you see in a lead that indicate genuine opportunity. CRM signals, website signals, TripAdvisor signals, deal history.
how_to_expand.negative_signals: Things that indicate low fit, bad timing, or a lead to deprioritise. Be honest — this helps Claude stop wasting your time on dead ends.
how_to_expand.website_mismatches: Patterns you see when someone's website doesn't match their stated booking setup. Each one should include what it means and how to use it in conversation.
how_to_expand.competitor_software: Any booking software you see in the wild. Add new ones as you encounter them.
how_to_expand.buying_triggers: Events or realisations that tend to move a prospect from cold to interested. These feed the 'why now' framing.
how_to_expand.objection_responses: Objections you hear and what works to reframe them. Claude uses these to shape the angle and draft.

pain_points: high OTA commission dependency (20-30% per booking)
low direct booking rate despite strong TripAdvisor presence
no direct booking widget on website
Book Now CTA on site but no booking system detectable behind it
poor conversion of web traffic to bookings
peak season capacity not being captured in advance
competitor booking software that lacks international distribution
no channel manager — managing inventory separately across platforms
double-booking risk from manual or split inventory management
no visibility on where bookings are coming from (OTA vs direct vs walk-in)
losing bookings to competitor operators who have slicker direct booking flows
guest communication is entirely manual (confirmation emails, reminders)
no upsell or add-on capability in their current booking flow
relying on phone bookings from international travellers across time zones
positive_signals: existing booking software (easier switch than starting from scratch)
previous demo or conversation in CRM
Book Now CTA present — intent to take online bookings, even if system is missing or broken
competitor software detected on site — confirms they are open to using booking technology
active on social media with high engagement — marketing-minded operator
multiple tours or products — complexity that benefits from a proper system
international-facing website (multi-currency, multiple languages) — high-value international bookers
listed on multiple OTAs — already managing multi-channel, would benefit from centralised inventory
Viator or GetYourGuide as primary channel — commission leakage is quantifiable and real
email address for owner or GM available in CRM
phone number available and not DNC flagged
recent rebrand or new website — they're investing in their business
negative_signals: business listed as for sale or being sold
explicit 'happy with current software' objection with no follow-up trigger
90%+ walk-in business with no online presence
no activity in CRM for 6+ months with no context
operator has explicitly said they don't want to change systems
business closed or seasonal with no reopening date confirmed
no identifiable decision maker in CRM
previous lost deal marked as 'not a fit' with no new trigger since

website_mismatches.signal: Book Now CTA with no booking system detectable
website_mismatches.what_it_means: They want online bookings but either have no system, a broken flow, or an undetectable redirect. High-value gap.
website_mismatches.how_to_use: Surface it gently as an observation — ask what is happening behind the button, not whether they need help.

website_mismatches.signal: No booking capability at all (no widget, no CTA)
website_mismatches.what_it_means: Business is taking bookings offline. International pre-arrival visitors have no way to book direct.
website_mismatches.how_to_use: Frame in terms of what a potential customer sees when they try to book — not as a flaw, but as a gap worth understanding.

website_mismatches.signal: Competitor software on site (Rezdy, Checkfront, Bokun, etc.)
website_mismatches.what_it_means: They use booking software. May be underserved on international distribution or direct booking share.
website_mismatches.how_to_use: Ask how it is working for them — specifically around direct vs OTA split. Do not pitch against the software directly.

website_mismatches.signal: OTA review badges prominent, no direct booking widget
website_mismatches.what_it_means: They are sending booking intent to OTAs via their own website. Every Viator click costs them 20-30% commission.
website_mismatches.how_to_use: Good supporting signal. Rarely a standalone angle — pair with a CRM signal or TripAdvisor presence.

website_mismatches.signal: CRM records one software, website shows different software
website_mismatches.what_it_means: CRM data may be stale, or they have switched systems. Worth clarifying before making assumptions.
website_mismatches.how_to_use: Note as a gap. Do not lead with it — use it to ask a clarifying question if the conversation opens.

website_mismatches.signal: Website appears unmaintained or very outdated
website_mismatches.what_it_means: May indicate a less tech-savvy operator. Could be an easy win if they have no system, or a harder sell if they see no value in online presence.
website_mismatches.how_to_use: Don't lead with this. Check their TripAdvisor and CRM activity — volume there may offset the weak web presence.

buying_triggers: seeing their OTA commission total for the season and doing the maths on what direct would have meant
a competitor switching to FareHarbor and visibly improving their direct booking flow
peak season sellout via OTAs with realisation that direct margin was left on the table
new ownership — fresh eyes, no attachment to existing setup
bad experience with current software (downtime, poor support, missed bookings)
new product launch or rebrand where they're rebuilding the website anyway
an international group booking that was hard to process without a proper system
someone asking them at a tourism conference who they use for bookings
a bad peak season where bookings were lower than expected and they're looking for what to change

objection_responses.what_it_means: Timing issue, not a no. Respect it.
objection_responses.reframe: Completely understand — when does your season wind down? I'd love to circle back in [April/May] when you have more headspace. Would that work?

objection_responses.what_it_means: They haven't felt enough pain to change. They haven't been shown what's possible.
objection_responses.reframe: That's great — how are you finding the direct booking share vs OTA volume? Most operators we talk to are running 60-70% OTA. Are you seeing something similar?

objection_responses.objection: We tried a different system before and it didn't work
objection_responses.what_it_means: Past bad experience. Find out what went wrong specifically.
objection_responses.reframe: Sorry to hear that — what happened? Understanding what didn't work helps me be honest about whether this would actually be different for you.

objection_responses.what_it_means: Either they don't understand the model, or the value hasn't landed yet.
objection_responses.reframe: I get that. The way FareHarbor works, there's no monthly fee — you pay per booking. So the question is really whether the direct bookings you gain outweigh that cost. Can we look at what your current OTA commission is running at?

objection_responses.objection: We mostly do walk-ins, we don't need online bookings
objection_responses.what_it_means: May be true. Worth validating rather than pushing.
objection_responses.reframe: That makes sense for some operators. What percentage of your bookings would you say are walk-in vs pre-booked? I ask because even at 30% pre-booked, the international visitor piece can add up.
